title: Async Context, Abort Signals, Deadlines, and Cleanup · Compiler Case: Unhandled Rejection Policy
summary: Request context is stored globally, promises continue after disconnect, timeouts race without cancellation, dependency budgets consume cleanup time, and rejected work becomes unhandled. Planned artifact: Add and verify the httpsts unhandled rejection policy increment in request context, budget, cancellation, and cleanup ledger. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Own background promise lifetimes, observe rejection and multiple-resolution failures, fail safely, and distinguish crash recovery from request error handling.
Release clients, streams, files, locks, timers, listeners, and spans exactly once across success, rejection, abort, timeout, and shutdown.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Node and Express server Predict Unhandled Rejection Policy · Theory Unhandled Rejection Policy
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory team handles case 6784 in a browser application using cookie authentication across approved origins. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to reserve cleanup time and combine client abort with one dependency budget; preserve one tenant fairness invariant; then change the tenant and prove denial before repository access. Browser code models pure decisions only. Node, Express, listener, TLS, PostgreSQL, proxy, worker, load, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Own background promise lifetimes, observe rejection and multiple-resolution failures, fail safely, and distinguish crash recovery from request error handling.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Unhandled Rejection Policy contract for the supplied data.
